phase_metadata.description:
This phase introduces the Java ecosystem, its history,
architecture, and the fundamental structure of a Java program.

learning_objectives:
Understand the history
and evolution of Java
Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM

Understand
the Java compilation and execution process

Set up the Java development
environment
Create and run a 'Hello World' program

Understand the
structure of the main method
Distinguish between print and println methods
prerequisites:
Ability to navigate file
systems and terminal/command prompt
introduction:
Welcome to the world of Java! Java is one of the most
popular, versatile, and enduring programming languages in history. Developed
with the philosophy of 'Write Once, Run Anywhere' (WORA), Java allows developers
to build robust, secure, and cross-platform applications ranging from mobile
apps to enterprise-scale banking systems.

concept_explanation.history:
Java was created by James Gosling and his team at Sun Microsystems in the
mid-1990s. Initially called 'Oak', it was later renamed Java. In 2010, Oracle
Corporation acquired Sun Microsystems and has since been the primary steward of
the language, releasing regular updates like Java 21 (LTS).

components.JVM:
The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) is the heart of Java. It is an engine
that provides a runtime environment to drive the Java code. It converts Java
bytecode into machine language that the computer's processor understands.
components.JRE:
The Java Runtime Environment (JRE) is a set of software tools which are
used for developing Java applications. It combines the JVM, platform core
classes, and supporting libraries. It's what you need to run Java programs.
components.JDK:
The Java Development Kit (JDK) is a full-featured software development
kit. It contains everything in the JRE, plus development tools like the compiler
(javac) and debugger. You need this to write and compile Java programs.

concept_explanation.workflow:
1. Write source code in a file ending with '.java'. 2. Compile the
code using 'javac', which creates a '.class' file containing Bytecode. 3. The
JVM executes this Bytecode on any platform that has a JRE installed.
concept_explanation.first_program_elements:
In Java, every line of code must reside inside a
'class'. The 'main' method is the entry point of every Java application. Without
it, the JVM will not know where to start executing your code.

breakdown_of_syntax.public:
An access modifier that means this class or
method is accessible from anywhere.
breakdown_of_syntax.class:
A keyword used to declare a
class in Java.
breakdown_of_syntax.ClassName:
The name of the class, which must exactly match
the filename (e.g., HelloWorld.java).
breakdown_of_syntax.static:
A keyword that allows the
main method to run without creating an instance (object) of the class.
breakdown_of_syntax.void: The return type of the method, meaning this method does not return any value.
breakdown_of_syntax.main: The specific name of the starting method required by the JVM.
breakdown_of_syntax.String[] args:
An array of strings used for command-line arguments passed to
the program.
breakdown_of_syntax.System.out.println:
A built-in command that prints text to the
console followed by a new line.

rules_and_key_points:
Java is
case-sensitive (e.g., 'Main' is different from 'main').

The class name must
match the filename exactly.
Every statement must end with a semicolon (;).
Code blocks are defined by curly braces { }.

The 'main' method signature
must be exactly: public static void main(String[] args).

Java code is compiled into
'Bytecode' (.class files), not machine code.

System.out.println() moves the
cursor to the next line after printing.

System.out.print() keeps the cursor
on the same line after printing.

code_example_1.title:
The Classic
Hello World
code_example_1.description:
A simple program to display a message on the
screen.

title: Understanding Output Methods
description:
Demonstrating the
difference between print() and println().
code: Mismatching the class name and the filename.

Forgetting the semicolon (;) at
the end of statements.

Using a lowercase 's' for 'System' or 'String' (Java
is case-sensitive).
Missing the 'static' keyword in the main method.
Writing code outside of class braces.
best_practices:
Always use
PascalCase for class names (e.g., MyFirstProgram).

Use meaningful names for
classes even in practice.

Indent your code properly using 4 spaces or a tab
for readability.
Add comments to explain the purpose of your code.

Keep
your JDK updated to the latest LTS version (currently 21).

frequently_asked_questions.question:
Do I need to install Java to run
a Java program?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
Yes, you need the JRE to run it, but as a
developer, you should install the JDK, which includes the JRE.

frequently_asked_questions.question: What is Bytecode?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
Bytecode is a highly optimized set of
instructions designed to be executed by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). It is
the '.class' file.

summary:
Phase 1 covered the architectural
foundations of Java. We learned that the JDK is our toolkit, the JVM is our
engine, and Java's platform independence comes from its unique compilation
process into bytecode. We also wrote our first program and explored the
structure of a Java class.
key_takeaways:
Java was created by James
Gosling.
JDK is for development; JRE is for running.

The file extension is .java; compiled extension is
.class.
The main method is the entry point.

Classes are the building blocks of
Java.

Java 21 is the current standard LTS
version.
preview_of_the_next_topic:
In Phase 2 - Basics, we will dive
into how Java stores data using Variables, the various Data Types available, and
how to perform calculations using Operators.

questions_and_answers.question:
What is the difference
between JDK and JRE?
questions_and_answers.answer:
The JDK (Java Development Kit) is a full
software development environment used to create Java applications; it includes a
compiler and debugger. The JRE (Java Runtime Environment) is the portion of the
software needed to run those applications. Essentially, JDK = JRE + Development
Tools.

questions_and_answers.question:
How does
Java achieve 'Write Once, Run Anywhere'?
questions_and_answers.answer:
Java achieves this by not
compiling code directly into machine-specific instructions. Instead, the Java
compiler (javac) converts source code into an intermediate format called
Bytecode (.class files). Any device with a Java Virtual Machine (JVM) installed
can interpret and execute this Bytecode, regardless of the underlying hardware
or operating system.

questions_and_answers.question: A
B
C
questions_and_answers.answer:
The output will be:
AB
C
Explanation: 'A' is printed and the
cursor stays. 'B' is printed on the same line as 'A', then 'println' moves the
cursor to the next line. 'C' is then printed on that new line.

questions_and_answers.question:
What happens if you
remove 'String[] args' from the main method signature and try to run the
program?
questions_and_answers.answer:
The code will compile successfully because the syntax is
valid for a method. However, at runtime, the JVM will throw a
'NoSuchMethodError'. The JVM specifically looks for a method named 'main' with a
'String array' parameter to use as the entry point of the application.
